title: Elattostachys apetala
taxonomy: Elattostachys apetala is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Sapindaceae, commonly known as the soapberry family. This family consists of over 130 genera and approximately 2,000 species of trees and shrubs. Elattostachys apetala is native to New Caledonia, a French territory in the Pacific Ocean. The classification of Elattostachys apetala is further divided into its genus and species, providing a clear understanding of its botanical identity. The Sapindaceae family is diverse and widespread, with members found in tropical and subtropical regions around the world.

intro: Elattostachys apetala, also known as the New Caledonian tree or the no-fruit tree, is a species of flowering plant in the family Anacardiaceae. It has been traditionally used in folk medicine due to its reported health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, which have been attributed to its ability to aid in wound healing and reduce fever. The bioactive constituents of Elattostachys apetala include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and triterpenoid saponins, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Elattostachys apetala is typically prepared as a decoction or a tincture, often used to treat various ailments, including skin conditions and respiratory issues.
